summary: DHH reflects on the challenge of finishing Basecamp 1.0 with just five days left before their January launch deadline. He notes that when the development team members are also genuine users of their own product, it becomes extremely difficult to resist the temptation of endless small improvements and tweaks. The post captures the tension between shipping on time and the natural desire to polish a product you personally use.
key_points: Feature creep is hardest to resist when developers are genuine users of their own product
Gold-plating through continuous tweaks and small additions threatens launch deadlines
Self-imposed shipping deadlines create necessary urgency to counteract perfectionism
money_quotes: When both designer and programmer are natural customers of their own product, it's incredibly hard to resist gold-plating — the continuous stream of tweaks and small additions.
key_insight: Dogfooding your own product makes feature creep especially dangerous because every user frustration feels like a must-fix before launch.
